literal: knowledge, learning, a discipline of knowing; from √vid 'to know'
invariant_definition: *Vidyā* is not 'knowledge' in the thin modern sense of possessing true propositions. In Sanskrit scholastic usage it can name a science, a taught discipline, an Upaniṣadic meditation-program, or the liberating cognition toward which such disciplines are ordered. It therefore sits inside the larger *pramāṇa* apparatus: a *vidyā* is normatively valid disclosure, often stabilized through recitation, hearing, reflection, and contemplative repetition, not raw information. In the Upaniṣadic and Brahma-Sūtra register one must distinguish named contemplative *vidyā*s such as *dahara-vidyā*, *madhu-vidyā*, and *pañcāgni-vidyā* from the culminating *brahma-vidyā*; much of *Brahma-Sūtra* 3.3 is about whether these are to be combined, repeated, or treated as alternatives. Advaita makes liberating *vidyā* a sublating cognition that cancels *adhyāsa*; Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) integrate it with sustained *upāsanā*, *bhakti*, and divine disclosure; Mīmāṃsā uses the contrast between injunctive *karma* passages and contemplative or explanatory passages to define its scope. The term belongs as much to disciplined practice as to semantics.

per_school.definition: Brahma-vidyā is a *nirvikalpaka-mano-vṛtti* whose object is the non-dual self alone, and it is therefore sharply distinguished from affect-laden *bhakti* as a distinct *sādhana* with its own *mahāvākya*-source [[BR 1.1 ṭīkā](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1)]. The Veda is the operative *pramāṇa* for this knowledge, since the *mahāvākya* is single in purport and teaches the self's non-duality through sentence-meaning [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)]. Vidyā is thus the direct *virodhinī* of *ayathā-vastv-avidyā* and does not admit *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* [[NS 1.55-56](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.55-56)].

The Vedāntic corpus is said to be set in motion for disclosing the self distinct from body, the *antaryāmin* Brahman, his *upāsanā*, and the unsurpassed Brahman-experience that follows the manifestation of the self's own nature, so *vidyā* is already structured as contemplative realization rather than bare predication [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. The same locus makes the Brāhmaṇa's desire-to-know, Vedic recitation, sacrifice, gift, austerity, and the *brahmavid*'s attainment of the supreme part of one ordered discipline, yielding a brahma-vidyā inseparable from *upāsanā* and culminating in *anavadhikātiśayānanda-brahmānubhava* [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)].

In Tattva-vāda, `vidyā` is Hari-bestowed right knowledge ordered to the soul's recognition of its paratantra status and the reality of difference; Madhva explicitly denies that mokṣa follows from the bare nature of knowledge apart from Viṣṇu's dispensation (cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15). As the Anuvyākhyāna aligns vairāgya, firm bhakti, upāsanā, and finally aparokṣa of Viṣṇu, brahma-vidyā functions as a devotional-cognitive discipline consummated in direct realization of Viṣṇu rather than in abheda-cognition, and abheda-vākyas are therefore read through sādṛśya rather than svarūpābheda (cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.3.1, cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69).

Śrī-Kṛṣṇa-saṅkīrtana is called *vidyā-vadhū-jīvanam*, the very life of the bride named *vidyā*, so knowing is figured as vivified and made fruitful when consciousness is turned through the divine name toward Bhagavān [[Śikṣ 1](cite://caitanya/shikshashtakam/1)]. The same verse binds that enlivened vidyā to *ceto-darpaṇa-mārjana*, nectar-savouring, and *sarvātma-snapana*, placing liberative disclosure within nāma-bhajana and devotional relish rather than outside them [[Śikṣ 1](cite://caitanya/shikshashtakam/1)].

In Dvaitādvaita, `vidyā` is yathārtha-vijñāna of a reality whose cit, acit, and Īśvara stand in svābhāvika-bhedābheda; cognition is not falsified by plurality, because every entity is knowable as brahmātmaka while remaining threefoldly articulated (cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/7, cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1). Its soteriological form is constant upāsanā of Kṛṣṇa for the removal of the darkness of ajñāna, so brahma-vidyā is contemplative realization under bhagavat-prasāda rather than bare propositional grasp (cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/6, cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1).

Brahman-knowledge removes ignorance and accomplishes *svarūpa-yogyatā* with respect to the attainment of Puruṣottama, so vidyā is a real purifier of the soul's fitness for divine attainment [[BSB 1.1.12-19a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19a)]. Yet Puruṣottama-prāpti is completed only when the jīva is accepted as the Lord's own and *bhakti-bhāva* effects the further *sahakāri-yogyatā*, so brahma-vidyā stands within a grace-ordered devotional economy rather than exhausting the whole soteriological process [[BSB 1.1.12-19b](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19b)].

For Sāṃkhya, the functional analogue of `vidyā` is samyagjñāna or viveka: a non-errant discriminative cognition generated by sustained tattvābhyāsa and articulated through the negating discernment `neti neti` (cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/64, cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/3.76). This knowledge alone effects apavarga, since bondage belongs to viparyaya and, once right knowledge is attained, dharma and the other causal factors no longer generate fresh bondage apart from the residual force of saṃskāra (cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/44, cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/67).

In Yoga, the analogue of `vidyā` is aviplava vivekakhyāti, the stable discernment of sattva and puruṣa that serves as the direct upāya of hāna (cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26, cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.26). When intensified through saṃyama it becomes vivekaja-jñāna, tāraka, sarvaviṣaya, and akrama, and this discriminative illumination culminates in kaivalya through the parity of purity between sattva and puruṣa (cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.52, cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.54, cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.55).

In the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika synthesis, `vidyā` is veridical cognition within the pramāṇa-governed acquisition of tattvajñāna, and such knowledge is explicitly linked to niḥśreyasa rather than to any non-epistemic self-manifestation (cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.1, cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.4). Vātsyāyana further treats the path as study and repeated exercise of the ātmavidyāśāstra together with dialogue with those who know it, since hearing, reflection, and clarification ripen prajñā and cut saṃśaya (cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.46, cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.47).

Śūnyatā is presented as something that must be mastered like a *vidyā*: when badly seen, it destroys the dull-minded just as a badly seized snake or a *duṣprasādhitā vidyā* destroys its handler [[MMK 24.11](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.11)]. Vidyā here is therefore a perilous corrective whose efficacy depends upon right *dṛṣṭi* and exact pedagogical handling, not a reified doctrinal possession [[MMK 24.11](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.11)].

In Yogācāra, the functional analogue of `vidyā` is lokottara-jñāna attained when cognition abides in vijñaptimātratā and ceases to grasp a distinct grāhya; that non-apprehending cognition is explicitly said to be supramundane (cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/28, cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/29). Its soteriological mark is āśraya-parāvṛtti, the reversal of the basis through the removal of dauṣṭhulya, by which the afflicted stream of consciousness is transformed into the stable, anāsrava condition described in the poem's conclusion (cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/29, cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/30).

In Pratyabhijñā / Trika, `vidyā` is the śuddha cognition in which `aham` and `idam` stand in sāmānādhikaraṇya, so that the world is recognized as resting in one consciousness rather than as an alien object-domain (cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.1.3). As vidyā-śakti it is the revealer of the paśu's obscured aiśvarya, opposed to māyā's tirodhāna, and liberation is accordingly the condition of the pramātṛ whose lordship has been made manifest by vidyā (cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.1.7, cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.2).

In Jainism, the functional analogue of `vidyā` is samyag-jñāna within the triad of samyag-darśana, jñāna, and cāritra that constitutes the mokṣa-mārga, and knowledge is classified into determinate grades culminating in kevala-jñāna (cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.1, cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9). Kevala-jñāna arises with the destruction of moha and of the karmic veils over knowledge and vision, so the liberative efficacy of vidyā is inseparable from karmic de-obstruction rather than exhausted by bare intellection (cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.1, cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.2).

per_school.not_applicable_reason: `vidyā` is not, in the present glossary sense, a distinct school-defining doctrinal category of the Pāṇinian-Vaiyākaraṇa tradition comparable to terms such as śabda, sphoṭa, or vākya.

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'knowledge.' That imports a modern picture in which a knower stores correct propositions. *Vidyā* often names a transformative discipline or contemplative realization, not a detachable content-item. Translating every occurrence as 'knowledge' also hides the difference between a named Upaniṣadic *vidyā*, liberating *brahma-vidyā*, and ordinary subject-matter learning.
